document_metadata.title: Platform System Architecture — Software Engineering Patterns & Technical Design Reference
document_metadata.purpose: Identifies the software engineering patterns, code architecture decisions, system design principles, and infrastructure patterns used to build a multi-tenant enterprise SaaS platform. All domain-specific terminology has been removed. What remains is a pure technical blueprint that could apply to any complex, hierarchical, event-driven, multi-tenant application.
document_metadata.intended_audience: Agentic coding agents, software architects, platform engineers, and developers building or extending systems with similar complexity requirements.

system_classification.type: Multi-tenant SaaS platform with layered security, graph-based orchestration, and RESTful integration layer
system_classification.scale_profile: Enterprise-grade: hundreds of tenants, thousands of users per tenant, millions of records, 40+ entity types, 10+ hierarchical tree structures
system_classification.architectural_style: Modular monolith with resource-oriented API surface, event-driven subsystem integration, and graph-based workflow orchestration engine

multi_tenancy_architecture.description: Every API request includes a tenant identifier in the URL path. The tenant namespace acts as the isolation boundary — all data, configuration, security rules, and entity graphs are scoped to a single tenant. There is no cross-tenant data leakage at any layer.

implementation.isolation_mechanism: Logical isolation via tenant-scoped queries (not separate databases per tenant). The tenant ID is resolved from the URL and applied as a filter to every data access operation.
implementation.configuration_isolation: Each tenant has independent: role hierarchies, feature trees, organizational hierarchies, password policies, workflow definitions, and entity configurations

oauth2_ropc_flow.pattern_name: OAuth 2.0 Resource Owner Password Credentials (ROPC) with JWT
oauth2_ropc_flow.description: Consumers exchange long-lived credentials for short-lived JWT tokens. The token is opaque to the consumer and encodes tenant scope, user identity, and expiration.
oauth2_ropc_flow.design_tradeoff: Non-revocable tokens trade immediate revocation capability for stateless verification (no server-side token store needed). This is a conscious architectural choice that simplifies the auth infrastructure at the cost of requiring short token lifetimes.

hybrid_authentication.description: The system supports SSO via external identity providers AND native credential management simultaneously. Individual accounts or entire roles can be configured to allow fallback to native auth when the SSO provider is unavailable.

mfa_pipeline.pattern_name: Pluggable Second-Factor Pipeline with Failure Mode Configuration
mfa_pipeline.description: MFA is implemented as a pluggable pipeline where multiple second-factor methods can be configured in priority order. Critically, the system has an explicit failure mode configuration: when the MFA provider is unavailable, the system can either deny all logins (closed mode) or degrade to single-factor (open mode). This is configurable per-policy.

password_policy_engine.description: Multiple password policies coexist. Each has a priority score. When evaluating which policy applies to a user, the engine sorts all matching policies by priority and applies the strongest match. This is a general pattern for any system where multiple overlapping policies must be resolved to a single effective policy.

authorization_architecture.core_principle: Security is subtractive by default. A principal has ZERO access until every independent layer explicitly grants permission. The effective access is the INTERSECTION of all layer grants, not the union.

layers.description: Requests are accepted or rejected based on source IP address before any application logic executes.
layers.implementation: IP range whitelist evaluated at the network/reverse-proxy layer

layers.description: The system verifies the principal's identity via credentials, tokens, or federated assertions.

layers.technical_name: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with Hierarchical Inheritance
layers.description: Each principal is assigned one or more roles organized in a parent-child tree. Child roles inherit constraints from parents and can NEVER exceed parent permissions. The system resolves the effective role set at request time.
layers.key_constraint: Monotonically subtractive inheritance — children can only narrow, never widen, parent permissions

layers.description: Application capabilities are organized as a tree of features (screens, modules, tabs, toggles). Each role is granted access to a curated subset. Only explicitly enabled features (and their children) are available. This is a whitelist — anything not granted is denied.
layers.data_structure: Tree (adjacency list with parent references). Two node types: container nodes (grouping) and leaf nodes (functional capabilities).

layers.description: Independent of feature access, each role has Create/Read/Update/Delete permissions on categories of data. A principal may access a feature (screen) but be restricted from certain data types within it. Permissions are organized as a matrix: roles × data categories × CRUD operations.

For API/integration channels specifically, individual fields within data entities can be independently enabled or disabled per role. This is implemented as a checkbox tree of every API-exposed field. Disabled fields are stripped from API responses regardless of broader authorization grants.

Data visibility is bounded by the principal's position in a multi-level organizational tree. Principals only see records belonging to organizational nodes they have been granted access to. Higher tree positions inherit visibility into all descendant nodes.

Even after a feature is enabled, its behavior can differ per role through configurable parameters (default views, time horizons, result limits, display options). The same feature renders differently based on role context.
All operations are logged with actor, action, target, timestamp, and outcome. Anomaly detection identifies suspicious patterns (failed login bursts, concurrent multi-IP sessions).

role_hierarchy_pattern.pattern_name: Tree-Structured RBAC with Monotonic Subtractive Inheritance
role_hierarchy_pattern.data_structure: Rooted tree (adjacency list: each role has 0 or 1 parent)
role_hierarchy_pattern.inheritance_rule: A child node can only reference permissions that exist on its parent. Administrators can only modify roles at or below their own level. The root node (no parent) has full administrative capability.

lifecycle_roles.description: The system defines special-purpose roles for lifecycle state transitions: pre-activation (before first login), self-service (restricted to own data), and deactivation (auto-assigned when account is disabled).
lifecycle_roles.pattern: State Machine on Principal Lifecycle → Automatic Role Transition

operational_patterns: Least Privilege — grant minimum required permissions
Just-In-Time Access — temporary privilege escalation with automatic revocation
Separation of Duties — isolate security admin from business admin roles
Periodic Access Review — scheduled audit of role-to-principal assignments

entity_identity_pattern.pattern_name: Dual-Key Identity: Internal Surrogate + External Cross-Reference Code
entity_identity_pattern.description: Every entity has TWO identifiers: (1) an internal auto-generated surrogate key (used for joins and relational integrity) and (2) an external cross-reference code (XRefCode) — a tenant-defined string that serves as the stable API-level identity. The XRefCode is the ONLY identifier exposed through the API. Internal IDs never leak to consumers.
constraints: XRefCode must be unique within entity type per tenant
Immutable after certain lifecycle events (e.g., after first workflow execution)
entity_identity_pattern.design_rationale: Decouples the internal data model from the integration contract. Internal IDs can be auto-incremented, relocated, or resequenced without breaking external integrations. XRefCodes are under tenant control, enabling meaningful, predictable identifiers.

temporal_data_model.description: Most entities support bi-temporal modeling: an EffectiveFrom/EffectiveTo date range that defines when the record is 'active' in the business sense. The system renders active/inactive views based on whether the current date falls within the effective range. Future-dated records can be created (pre-staging), and historical records are retained.

query_semantics.point_in_time: ContextDate parameter returns the record effective as of that date
query_semantics.range_query: ContextDateRangeFrom/To returns all records effective within a window
query_semantics.default: Current server datetime if no ContextDate specified

implications: Every query is implicitly temporal — there is no 'current' record, only 'effective as of date X'
Insert operations create a new effective-dated version, not an overwrite
Delete is expressed as end-dating (setting EffectiveTo), not physical removal
Schema requires: effective_from DATETIME NOT NULL, effective_to DATETIME NULL on most tables

composite_aggregate_pattern.pattern_name: Aggregate Root with Subordinate Resource Collections
composite_aggregate_pattern.description: The central entity (aggregate root) is a composite of a master record and 40+ child collections (subordinate resources). Each child collection contains independently effective-dated records. The aggregate is addressable at the root level (/Entity/{id}) or at the child collection level (/Entity/{id}/{SubResource}).

access_patterns.selective_expansion: The 'Expand' parameter controls which child collections are loaded in a single request (lazy loading by default, eager loading on demand)
access_patterns.sub_resource_direct_access: Child collections can be addressed directly via sub-resource URLs without loading the full aggregate
access_patterns.symmetric_payloads: The JSON structure returned by GET is the same structure accepted by POST/PATCH (read and write share the same schema)

tree_data_structures.pattern_name: Multiple Independent Tree Hierarchies (Adjacency List Model)
tree_data_structures.description: The system maintains at least 5 independent tree structures, each stored using the adjacency list pattern (each node holds a reference to its parent). Trees serve different purposes but share the same storage and traversal infrastructure.

trees.name: Organizational Hierarchy
trees.purpose: Data visibility scoping and record ownership
trees.characteristics: Multi-level (configurable depth), nodes typed by 'level', effective-dated parent assignments, supports reparenting

trees.name: Role Hierarchy
trees.characteristics: Single-root, monotonic subtractive inheritance, max 1 parent per node

trees.name: Feature Tree
trees.characteristics: Two node types (container, leaf), whitelist semantics, parent must be enabled before children

trees.name: Supervisory / Reporting Hierarchy
trees.purpose: Approval routing and management chain traversal
trees.characteristics: Position-based (independent of the people occupying positions), supports solid-line and dotted-line relationships

trees.name: Functional Unit Hierarchy
trees.characteristics: Aggregate metrics (count, filled, vacant) roll up through the tree

traversal_algorithms.upward_walk_with_early_termination: Starting from a leaf node, walk up toward the root, evaluating a predicate at each level. Stop at the FIRST level where the predicate is satisfied. This is the core routing algorithm used by the workflow engine.
traversal_algorithms.downward_visibility_propagation: A principal at level N inherits visibility into all nodes at levels N+1, N+2, ... (all descendants).
traversal_algorithms.effective_date_aware_traversal: Parent-child relationships are themselves effective-dated, so the tree shape can change over time. Traversals must resolve relationships as-of a specific date.

tree_data_structures.storage_model: Adjacency list (parent_id FK on each node). NOT nested sets or materialized paths, based on the operational characteristics (frequent reparenting, effective dating on edges).

reference_data_pattern.description: The system maintains a set of reference data entities (codebooks) that define the valid values for categorized fields across the data model. Each codebook entity follows the same structure: XRefCode, ShortName, LongName. These are the enumerations of the system — but they're tenant-configurable, not hardcoded.
reference_data_pattern.access_pattern: Same two-step retrieval as domain entities: list XRefCodes, then get detail by XRefCode

document_storage_pattern.pattern_name: BLOB Storage with Base64 API Serialization
document_storage_pattern.description: Binary documents are stored as BLOBs in the database. When retrieved through the API, they are serialized to Base64-encoded byte arrays within the JSON response. Consumers must deserialize on their side.
document_storage_pattern.metadata_model: Each document has: GUID (identifier), name, type category, upload timestamp, uploader, content (Base64), file size
document_storage_pattern.security: Document access is controlled at the type-category level, not the individual document level

polymorphic_properties_pattern.description: Entities support tenant-defined custom properties with multiple data types (string, number, boolean, date, pick list). These are stored using an EAV-style model where property definitions are separate from property values. The expression engine can evaluate these at runtime.

resource_oriented_design.description: Every domain entity is a first-class resource with a canonical URL. Operations map to HTTP verbs: GET (read), POST (create), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove). URLs follow a consistent hierarchical pattern.

two_phase_retrieval.description: Data retrieval is split into two phases: (1) a cheap, unsecured discovery call that returns only entity identifiers (XRefCodes), and (2) individual secured detail calls that return the full entity with all security layers applied. This pattern keeps the discovery phase fast while concentrating the security evaluation cost on the detail phase.

delta_sync_pattern.description: The API is designed around incremental synchronization. Consumers maintain a high-water mark (the timestamp of their last successful request) and query for entities that changed since that timestamp. The platform evaluates changes across the ENTIRE data model (not just the fields the consumer has access to) to determine which entities have changed.

change_detection_criteria: A currently-effective record was modified within the window
A record became effective (start date) within the window
A record ended (end date) within the window
mechanism.granularity_filter: Optional entity-type filter narrows change detection to specific sub-resource types

delta_sync_pattern.important_behavior: Change detection spans the FULL data model regardless of the consumer's access permissions. A change to a restricted field still surfaces the entity in the discovery phase — but the restricted field won't appear in the detail response. This prevents security-filtered changes from being invisible to the sync process.

query_governor_pattern.description: Detail retrieval operations enforce a hard maximum on the number of entities returned per request (1,000). Exceeding the limit returns an error, NOT truncated results. This forces consumers to implement client-side batching.
query_governor_pattern.consumer_pattern: Split the XRefCode list from Phase 1 into batches of ≤N (where N < governor limit), and make one Phase 2 request per batch

cursor_based_pagination.description: Large result sets use cursor-based pagination. Each response page includes a 'Paging.Next' field containing a full URL with an opaque cursor token. The consumer follows the chain of next-page URLs until the field is absent.
cursor_based_pagination.advantages_over_offset: Cursor-based pagination is stable under concurrent writes (no skipped/duplicated records when data changes between page requests)

rate_limiting.pattern_name: Tenant-Level Rate Limiting with Client-Side Backoff
rate_limiting.description: API requests are rate-limited at the tenant level (not per-consumer-application). All consuming applications for a single tenant share the same rate limit pool. Exceeded requests are rejected immediately (not queued).
consumer_requirements: Sequential processing (no overlapping requests for same data type)
Coordinated rate allocation when multiple consumers share a tenant

validate_only_mode.description: All write operations (POST, PATCH) accept an 'isValidateOnly' flag that runs the full server-side validation pipeline without committing any data. This enables consumers to test payloads against production validation rules without side effects.

symmetric_payload_design.description: The JSON structure returned by GET is the same structure accepted by POST and PATCH. Consumers can: (1) GET an entity, (2) modify fields in the response JSON, (3) send the modified JSON back as a PATCH body. No schema transformation is needed between read and write.
symmetric_payload_design.partial_update_support: PATCH bodies only need to include the fields being changed, not the complete entity

consumer_generated_identity.description: When creating a new entity via POST, the CONSUMER is responsible for generating and providing a unique XRefCode. The server rejects duplicates. This inverts the typical pattern where the server generates the ID and returns it.
consumer_generated_identity.design_rationale: Enables idempotent creation (consumer can retry a POST with the same XRefCode without creating duplicates) and maintains the principle that XRefCodes are tenant-controlled, not platform-controlled.

graph_engine.pattern_name: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) Execution Engine with Controlled Looping
graph_engine.description: Business process orchestration is modeled as a visual graph of typed nodes connected by directional links. The engine traverses the graph sequentially, executing each node's behavior and following the appropriate outbound link based on the node's output.

graph_properties.cycles: Controlled — a global max-iterations counter prevents infinite loops
graph_properties.node_typing: 9 distinct node types, each with specific input/output constraints
graph_properties.link_typing: Links carry response-type metadata (Accept, Reject, True, False, Timeout, etc.)
graph_properties.validation: Graph topology is validated on save — invalid structures are rejected

node_type_catalog.description: Each node type implements a specific behavioral contract. The type system enforces which nodes can connect to which other nodes.

types.behavior: Single entry node. Configures global graph constraints (max iterations, error routing).
types.technical_analog: Start state in a state machine

types.behavior: Marks graph completion. No outbound links.
types.technical_analog: Accept/final state in a state machine

types.behavior: Resolves one or more recipients dynamically by traversing hierarchical data structures at runtime. Does NOT pause execution.
types.technical_analog: Dynamic dispatch / service locator with hierarchy traversal

types.behavior: PAUSES execution and waits for human input. Multiple recipients may receive the prompt; the first response wins (competing consumer pattern). Supports configurable timeout with auto-resolution.
types.technical_analog: Human-in-the-loop gate / approval barrier with timeout and first-responder semantics

types.behavior: Commits pending data changes to the database. This is the node where staged data becomes persisted. Can be configured as approve, reject, or presave.
Sends fire-and-forget messages to resolved recipients. Does NOT pause execution.
Evaluates a boolean expression against the payload data. Binary branching (true/false). For multi-way branching, chain multiple condition evaluators.
Fires a domain event that triggers downstream graph executions. Represents a tight coupling point to other subsystems.
types.technical_analog: Webhook emission / domain event publication / system callback

types.behavior: Human-readable documentation node. Not part of execution flow.

execution_model.pattern_name: Sequential Node Evaluation with Pause-on-Gate Semantics
execution_model.description: The engine processes nodes sequentially. Most nodes execute instantly and advance to the next. Decision Gate nodes PAUSE execution and create pending work items for resolved recipients. Execution resumes when a response is received or a timeout fires.
execution_model.state_tracking: Per-graph execution history with per-node status tracking (pending, completed, timed out, bypassed, error)

dynamic_routing_engine.pattern_name: Hierarchy-Aware Dynamic Recipient Resolution with Early Termination
dynamic_routing_engine.description: The routing subsystem resolves recipients at RUNTIME (not design time) by traversing hierarchical data structures. Starting from a context entity, the algorithm walks up a tree structure, evaluating a predicate at each level, and stops at the FIRST level where a qualifying recipient is found.
routing_strategies: Role-relative traversal (walk hierarchy until a node with target role is found)
Authority-type relative traversal (walk until authority type match)
Supervisor-chain traversal with configurable depth (level 1 = direct, level 2 = skip-level, etc.)
Future-state resolution (resolve against a pending/uncommitted hierarchy change)
Submitter-relative routing (start traversal from the submitter's position instead of the affected entity)
dynamic_routing_engine.multi_hierarchy_search: Routing can search the supervisory hierarchy, the organizational hierarchy, or BOTH independently. A recipient found via either search receives the work item. A recipient found via both receives only one.
dynamic_routing_engine.fallback_behavior: Configurable: either continue to default recipient or fail with error when no match found

embedded_expression_engine.pattern_name: Embedded Domain-Specific Language (DSL) for Conditional Logic
embedded_expression_engine.description: The workflow engine includes a rich expression language used in condition evaluator nodes. Expressions evaluate payload data, system state, and entity collections to produce boolean results that drive branching logic.

capabilities.field_references: Token syntax (<<FieldName>>) references payload fields and related entity fields

collection_operations.All: Returns true if every item matches
collection_operations.None: Returns true if zero items match

built_in_functions: Current (currently-effective record from a temporal collection)
LoadIfEmpty (lazy-load system state not present in the payload)
capabilities.data_providers: 100+ registered data stores available for cross-reference lookups

timeout_and_auto_resolution.pattern_name: Configurable Timeout with Retry and Auto-Resolution
timeout_and_auto_resolution.description: Decision gate nodes support: (1) a timeout value in minutes, (2) retry count (0-10), (3) a timeout link that routes to an auto-resolution path. This prevents graphs from stalling indefinitely when no human response is received.
patterns: Escalation on timeout (Timeout → Router[escalated recipient] → Decision Gate)

first_responder_pattern.description: When a decision gate is routed to multiple recipients, the first person to respond advances the graph. All other pending decisions for that node are automatically cancelled. There is no aggregation of multiple responses.

immutability_pattern.description: Once a graph definition has been executed at least once, its topology (nodes, links, connections) is PERMANENTLY FROZEN. Only node properties (messages, routing targets, timeouts) can be modified. This protects the integrity of already-executed processes. To make structural changes, administrators must copy the graph to create a new draft.

event_chaining.description: Completed graph executions can fire events that trigger secondary graph executions. Events are configured as subscriptions: one or more triggering forms → one or more triggered graphs. Execution is SEQUENTIAL (not parallel) when multiple graphs subscribe to the same event.
event_chaining.import_triggered_events: Events can also be triggered by bulk data imports, with configurable lookback periods and once-per-record deduplication

form_binding_architecture.description: Data entry forms are the trigger mechanism for graph execution. The binding between form and graph is ROLE-SCOPED — the same form can trigger different graphs for different roles. Decision gate nodes can override which form is presented to recipients, enabling multi-view approval patterns.
form_binding_architecture.multi_form_orchestration: Multiple forms can be bundled into a guided process (wizard) with per-form graph binding, skip support, and lifecycle tracking

push_pull_notification_system.description: External systems can receive event notifications via two modes: (1) push — the platform sends events to a consumer-hosted endpoint, or (2) pull — the consumer polls the platform for unacknowledged events. Both modes require explicit acknowledgment to advance the event queue.
push_pull_notification_system.ordering_guarantee: Events are ordered per-subscriber. Cross-subscriber ordering is not guaranteed.
push_pull_notification_system.failure_isolation: A stalled subscriber blocks only its own event queue. Other subscribers are unaffected.

external_validation_hook.pattern_name: External System Participation in Approval Chains
external_validation_hook.description: The graph engine can pause at a validation node and emit an event to an external system. The external system evaluates the pending data against its own rules and responds with an action (approve, reject, etc.) that advances the graph. This enables external business rule engines to participate in the platform's approval process without modifying the graph engine itself.

message_template_engine.pattern_name: Token-Based Message Template System with Masking and Localization
message_template_engine.description: Notification messages use a template engine with <<Token>> syntax that resolves to payload fields at runtime. Supports: foreign key lookup resolution (Lookup function), sensitive data masking by default with explicit Unmask override, per-language localization with recipient-preferred language delivery, and rich text formatting.

background_job_processing.description: Certain administrative operations are executed asynchronously via background jobs: data synchronization, hierarchy integrity checks and auto-repair, automatic role reassignment on account status change, display format propagation.

configuration_portability.description: Role configurations (features, authorizations, parameters, field-level access) can be exported to a portable file format and imported into a different environment. Import behavior is upsert-based: creates if XRefCode doesn't exist, overwrites if it does. Never deletes.

import_export_framework.pattern_name: CSV-Based Bulk Import/Export with Header-Locked Schema
import_export_framework.description: The platform provides a consistent import/export pattern: export a CSV template with headers and existing records, modify offline, re-import. Import supports add and update but never delete. Field order is locked (headers must not be reordered). Reference codes in relational fields must match existing records.

audit_infrastructure.pattern_name: Universal Change Audit with Shadow Tables
audit_infrastructure.description: Every entity modification is logged with: actor (who), action (what changed — before and after values), target (which record), and timestamp (when). Audit records are viewable in dedicated UI sections throughout the platform. API calls are logged in separate audit tables with call metadata and outcome.

localization_framework.description: Names and descriptions for configurable entities support per-language localization. When a user creates or updates an entity in a non-default language, the change is stored as a localization for that language only. The system falls back to the organization's default language when a localization is unavailable.

encryption_model.pattern_name: Encryption at Rest + In Transit + PGP for Outbound Integration
encryption_model.description: Three encryption layers: (1) all stored data encrypted at rest, (2) all client-server communication encrypted in transit (TLS), (3) outbound integration data encrypted with PGP (public key per integration partner).
encryption_model.file_transfer_security: SFTP accounts with 90-day password rotation, per-user/per-vendor credentials, immediate rotation on personnel changes

data_lifecycle_management.description: Tenants configure data retention periods per data category. The platform automates purging of expired records. Supports data minimization (reducing stored data volume to limit breach exposure) and data subject access requests (privacy compliance).

design_pattern_cross_reference.description: A quick-reference mapping of industry-standard design patterns to where they appear in this architecture.

patterns.ABAC (partial): Authorization architecture → organizational scoping + data authorization adds attribute-based dimensions to the RBAC core
patterns.Repository Pattern: API design → two-phase retrieval (discovery = repository query, detail = aggregate loading)
patterns.Saga Pattern: Workflow engine → event chaining (sequential graph executions forming a distributed transaction)
patterns.State Machine: Workflow engine → graph execution model with states per node
patterns.Competing Consumers: Workflow engine → first-responder pattern on decision gates
patterns.Strategy Pattern: Workflow engine → routing strategies (different algorithms selected by configuration)
patterns.Chain of Responsibility: Authorization → layered security pipeline (each layer can deny; all must permit)
patterns.Template Method: Message templates → token interpolation with masking/unmasking overrides
patterns.Visitor Pattern: Workflow engine → graph traversal with type-specific node processing
patterns.Builder Pattern: Expression engine → chainable Filter().Any().All() collection operations
patterns.Facade Pattern: API layer → single REST surface over complex internal subsystems
patterns.Proxy Pattern: Field-level API access → strips unauthorized fields before response reaches consumer
patterns.Null Object: Workflow engine → continue-if-no-recipient-found option prevents null reference errors in routing
patterns.Flyweight Pattern: Reference data → shared codebook entities referenced by many records
patterns.Memento Pattern: Configuration portability → export/import captures and restores configuration state
patterns.Command Pattern: Write operations → POST/PATCH as commands with validate-only dry-run mode
patterns.Event Sourcing (partial): Audit infrastructure → before/after change logging creates a reconstructible event history
patterns.CQRS (partial): Two-phase retrieval → lightweight query (discovery) separated from full read model (detail)
patterns.Circuit Breaker: MFA pipeline → fail-open/fail-closed mode when auth provider is down
patterns.Bulkhead: Rate limiting → tenant-level isolation prevents one consumer from starving others
patterns.SCD Type 2: Temporal data model → effective-dated records preserving full history

anti_patterns_and_constraints.description: Architectural constraints and known limitations that an implementing agent must respect.

constraints.constraint: No server-generated identifiers exposed through API
constraints.implication: Consumers must generate and manage their own XRefCodes. Collision detection is the consumer's responsibility.
If a token is compromised, it remains valid until expiration. Compensating control: short token lifetime (1 hour).

constraints.constraint: Graph topology frozen after first execution
constraints.implication: Structural refactoring requires copy → modify → activate cycle. Cannot hotfix graph topology in production.
Long event chains execute serially. No fan-out parallelism for triggered graphs.

constraints.constraint: Query governor hard limit (1,000 per detail request)
constraints.implication: Client-side batching is mandatory for large datasets. The API is not designed for bulk extraction.
No consensus/quorum mechanism. Cannot require 'N of M approvers must agree.'

constraints.constraint: Binary branching only on condition evaluators
constraints.implication: Multi-way branching requires chaining multiple condition nodes in sequence (cascading if/else-if pattern).
Tree traversal algorithms must be time-aware. The same query at different ContextDates may traverse different tree shapes.

constraints.constraint: Report security uses a different path than entity security
constraints.implication: A consumer may see different fields via the report API than via the direct entity API, even with the same role. Two independent security configurations must be maintained.
